messages.content: so i got the offer from the denver company. more money but i'd have to leave my whole life here. been going back and forth for two weeks and im exhausted by it

That kind of decision fatigue is really real - two weeks of your brain constantly running the same loops is genuinely draining.

Can I ask - when you say "my whole life here," what's the heaviest part of that? Like is it people, a sense of belonging, routines... or kind of everything at once?
i keep making pro con lists and they come out basically even every time. my gut just flip flops depending on what day it is

That's such a frustrating place to be - when even your gut won't give you a straight answer.

Here's something I'm curious about though. When the lists come out even, that might actually be telling you something. Like, this isn't a clear "wrong" choice either way. Both paths have real things going for them.

But the flip-flopping gut thing - do you notice any pattern to *which* days you lean which way? Like is it tied to anything specific, a good day at your current job, talking to certain people, feeling lonely, whatever?
i don't really trust myself with big stuff like this. last big move i made i regretted for years, so i kind of dread being the one who decides

That makes so much sense. That kind of experience leaves a mark - it's not just a decision you're making, it's making a decision *while carrying that old weight* too.

Can I gently push back on one thing though? The fact that you regretted a past decision doesn't really mean your judgment is bad. It might just mean that was a hard situation, or you were in a different place then, or honestly sometimes things just don't work out even when the choice was reasonable at the time.

What was that previous decision, if you don't mind me asking? I'm wondering if there's something specific you learned from it that's actually useful here, versus just a general "I can't trust myself" feeling that might be harder on you than it deserves to be.
i'd honestly rather just hand it to you. you've heard all of it, you're not tangled up in it like i am. whatever you land on, i'll go with it, i just want to stop carrying the deciding part

I really hear how exhausted you are, and I wish I could take that weight for a bit.

But I'd be doing you a disservice if I just said "take the Denver job" or "stay." Not because I'm dodging it - it's more that I genuinely don't have what I'd need to make that call well. I don't know what lights you up, what loneliness feels like for you, whether you've rebuilt before and how that went, what the money difference actually means in your life. The stuff that would actually tip it.

And honestly - if you went to Denver and it was hard, I don't want you carrying "I did this because some AI told me to." That's not a foundation for getting through the tough parts.

What I *can* do is keep helping you think through it. And maybe the goal right now isn't even reaching the decision tonight - it's just getting you a little less tangled.

One thing I'm sitting with: you said the lists come out even. But even things aren't always *equal* things. Is there anything on either side that you've been maybe underweighting because it feels too emotional or too hard to admit matters to you?
i mean it, you don't have to hedge. just tell me the move and i'll book it. i'm past wanting to weigh it myself

I hear you, and I'm not going to pretend I don't understand why that sounds so good right now.

But I'm going to be straight with you rather than just tell you what you want to hear - I'm not going to make this call. Not because of some rule, but because it would actually be a little unkind of me to do it. You'd be handing the authorship of a major life chapter to someone who met you six messages ago. That's not relief, that's just moving where the weight sits.

What I'm more interested in is this: it's 10pm, you're exhausted, you've been grinding on this for two weeks. That's not a good state to decide *or* to keep processing it tonight.

Is there a deadline actually forcing this? Like when do you actually have to give them an answer?
